facts_disclosed: Switched to Rust for new platform

turns.content: quick question. is it normal to mass-delete your own code 3 times in one day because the compiler told you you're wrong about everything
Ha - sounds like you're having a fun time with a strict compiler. Which language is giving you a hard time?
rust. we switched to rust for the new platform at work and the borrow checker is personally victimizing me
Ah, the Rust borrow checker - famously unforgiving but famously right. How long have you been working with it? The first few months are usually the steepest part of the curve.

about 3 months. maybe a little more. i'm finally getting productive but there are still moments where i stare at a lifetime annotation like it's written in ancient sumerian. i used to be fast. now i am correct and slow. the ownership model is basically the borrow checker being a very strict german teacher — annoying in the moment but you grudgingly admit you learned something

"Correct and slow" is the perfect description of the Rust learning curve. The speed comes back - you're just front-loading all the debugging into compile time. Three months in and feeling productive is actually ahead of schedule.

What were you writing before Rust?

kotlin. jvm ecosystem for 4+ years. and look, the jvm is fine, coroutines are elegant, tooling is mature. but with the company pivot we decided to go full rust for the new services. performance requirements for the risk calculation engine basically demanded it. no gc pauses when you're doing real-time credit scoring

Kotlin to Rust is a huge paradigm jump, but for a risk calculation engine where latency matters, Rust is a strong choice. The hardest part of that transition is probably going from garbage-collected thinking to ownership thinking. How's the ecosystem treating you? Crate quality can be inconsistent.

crate ecosystem is honestly better than i expected. tokio for async, serde is incredible, sqlx for database stuff. cargo is better than gradle and i say that as someone who spent years configuring gradle builds. the compile times though. mein gott. i go make coffee, come back, still compiling

Cargo being better than Gradle is a strong statement from a Kotlin veteran, but most people who use both would agree. The compile times are the tax you pay for all those guarantees. `cargo check` and `sccache` can help a lot if you're not using them already.

Are you the only one on the team learning Rust or did everyone switch together?

3 of us switched. did a mini bootcamp for 2 weeks before touching real code. one engineer came from c++ so she's basically cheating — ownership is just her normal life. the rest of us jvm refugees are catching up. velocity is maybe 60-70% of what we had in kotlin but the code is tighter, fewer runtime surprises. the jvm isn't dead to me though. it's just resting. genau

60-70% velocity with dramatically fewer runtime errors is a great tradeoff at three months in. That velocity gap will close, but the reliability improvements are permanent. Having a C++ person on the team is a huge advantage - they can translate ownership concepts for the rest of you.

"Resting" is diplomatic. Most people who switch to Rust from the JVM don't go back voluntarily.

that's what i'm afraid of. becoming one of those rust evangelists who can't have a conversation without mentioning zero-cost abstractions. although — the error messages are genuinely good. the compiler doesn't just say 'no', it says 'no, and here's exactly why, and here's what you probably meant to do'. it's like pair programming with a very pedantic senior engineer

Rust's compiler messages are arguably its best feature for learners. Most compilers treat errors like punishment - Rust treats them like teaching moments. That "here's what you probably meant" suggestion system has probably saved millions of developer hours collectively. You're already halfway to evangelism if you're praising the error messages.

ugh you're right. okay i should get back to fighting the borrow checker. at least now i understand why it's yelling at me. progress. maybe by month six i'll stop flinching when i see lifetime annotations

Month six is usually when lifetimes click and you stop seeing them as obstacles. Good luck - and the fact that you're self-aware about potential evangelism means you'll probably just become quietly productive in Rust instead.
